title: Anxiety Thought Record
description: Use this worksheet to identify and challenge anxious thoughts using cognitive restructuring.
instructions: When you notice your anxiety rising, pause and work through each step below. Start by describing the situation, then identify the automatic thought that triggered your anxiety. Rate your emotions, identify any thinking traps, examine the evidence, and create a more balanced perspective.

fields.label: 1. Situation — What happened? Where were you? Who was there?
fields.placeholder: Describe the situation that triggered your anxiety...

fields.label: 2. Automatic Thought — What went through your mind?
fields.placeholder: What was the thought or image that popped into your head?

fields.label: 3. Emotions — How intense were your feelings? (0 = none, 100 = most intense)
4. Thinking Trap — Which cognitive distortion best fits?
5. Evidence For — What supports this thought?
fields.placeholder: What facts or experiences support this thought being true?

fields.label: 6. Evidence Against — What contradicts this thought?
fields.placeholder: What facts, past experiences, or alternative explanations contradict this thought?

fields.label: 7. Balanced Thought — What is a more realistic perspective?
fields.placeholder: Based on the evidence, write a more balanced and realistic thought...

fields.label: 8. Re-rate Emotions — How intense are your feelings now? (0 = none, 100 = most intense)

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Imagining the worst possible outcome and treating it as likely or inevitable.

distortionsList.name: Mind Reading
distortionsList.description: Assuming you know what others are thinking, usually something negative about you.

distortionsList.name: Fortune Telling
distortionsList.description: Predicting the future negatively without evidence to support the prediction.

distortionsList.name: Black-and-White Thinking
distortionsList.description: Seeing things in only two categories (all good or all bad) with no middle ground.

distortionsList.name: Overgeneralization
distortionsList.description: Drawing broad conclusions from a single event, using words like 'always' or 'never'.

distortionsList.name: Mental Filter
distortionsList.description: Focusing only on the negative details while ignoring the positive aspects of a situation.

distortionsList.name: Disqualifying the Positive
distortionsList.description: Dismissing positive experiences by insisting they 'don't count' for some reason.

distortionsList.name: Should Statements
distortionsList.description: Using 'should', 'must', or 'ought to' statements that create unrealistic expectations.

distortionsList.name: Labeling
distortionsList.description: Assigning a fixed, global label to yourself or others instead of describing the behavior.

distortionsList.name: Personalization
distortionsList.description: Blaming yourself for events outside your control, or taking things personally.

distortionsList.name: Magnification / Minimization
distortionsList.description: Exaggerating the importance of negative events or minimizing positive ones.

distortionsList.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Assuming that because you feel a certain way, it must be true ('I feel anxious, so something bad must be about to happen').

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for professional mental health treatment. If you are in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.
